question: Compare the Ch.01 overview of containment features with the Ch.05 detailed containment role in ECCS operation.

nuscale_ch01_0168.title: SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3: Fission Product Control Systems and StructuresII.2 Secondary Containment
nuscale_ch01_0168.summary: The NuScale design does not include secondary containment or fission product clean-up systems, as its passive safety features and submerged containment vessel inherently control fission products. Therefore, the regulatory criteria in SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3, related to secondary containment and certain fission product control systems, are not applicable to the NuScale Power Module. Only aspects related to containment leakage rates and isolation are relevant for review.

nuscale_ch01_0167.title: SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3: Fission Product Control Systems and StructuresII.1 Primary Containment
nuscale_ch01_0167.summary: The NuScale design partially conforms to SRP 6.5.3, Rev 3 requirements for fission product control systems, as its passive containment vessel does not include active fission product clean-up or pressure suppression systems, making some guidance inapplicable. The design does not have secondary containment or other credited engineered safety feature (ESF) fission product control systems, so those criteria are also not applicable. The review focuses on containment parameters such as leakage rates and isolation features.

nuscale_ch01_0189.title: BTP 6-2, Rev 3: Minimum Containment Pressure Model for PWR ECCS PerformanceAll (B.1 thru B.3)Various
nuscale_ch01_0189.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria related to containment pressure and water chemistry for PWR ECCS performance. It notes that some criteria partially apply, but specific requirements for systems like containment spray and sump are not relevant to NuScale’s design, as these features are absent. Additionally, guidance on minimum containment pressure and bypass leakage paths is deemed not applicable, since NuScale’s design does not result in core uncovery during a LOCA and lacks secondary containment.

nuscale_ch01_0188.title: BTP 6-1, (March 2007): pH for Emergency Coolant Water for Pressurized Water ReactorsB.3 Hydrogen Generation from Aluminum Corrosion
nuscale_ch01_0188.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria regarding pH control and hydrogen generation from aluminum corrosion in emergency coolant water for pressurized water reactors. While NuScale’s design lacks certain systems referenced in the criteria (such as containment spray systems and sumps), the intent of the requirements is still met through water collection and recirculation within the containment vessel. Some criteria, particularly those related to large LWRs with dual containment or core uncovery during LOCA, are not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, which operate without active power. The system vents steam into containment for condensation and recirculates water back to the core, with continued cooling achieved by heat conduction through the containment wall to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves—including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures, and in borated coolant—using test articles and high-pressure, high-temperature test cells. The tests also evaluated unique design features such as the inadvertent actuation block (IAB) and confirmed the operability of key valve components.

nuscale_ch01_0780.title: SRP 15.6.5.A, Rev 1: Radiological Consequences of a Design Basis Loss-of-Coolant Accident Including Containment Leakage ContributionII.1 Calculated Doses and Containment Leakage Contribution
nuscale_ch01_0780.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for evaluating the radiological consequences of a design basis loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA), including containment leakage. It notes that while NuScale’s analysis methods partially conform to SRP 15.6.5.A guidance, the acceptance criteria for radiological consequences are superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3, and some guidance applies only to BWRs. The section also confirms full conformance with requirements for engineered safety feature (ESF) system leakage assumptions.

nuscale_ch01_0779.title: DSRS 15.6.5, Rev 0: Loss-of-Coolant Accidents Resulting from Spectrum of Postulated Piping Breaks within the Reactor Coolant Pressure BoundaryII.4 Programmatic Requirements
nuscale_ch01_0779.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for loss-of-coolant accidents (LOCA) as outlined in DSRS 15.6.5 and related SRP sections. It notes full or partial conformance with requirements for calculating radiological consequences and containment leakage, with some criteria superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3 and certain guidance applicable only to BWRs. The table documents how NuScale addresses each regulatory requirement for LOCA analysis.

nuscale_ch01_0604.title: SRP 13.3, Rev 3: Emergency PlanningII.25 Design and Implementation of Emergency Response Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0604.summary: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report partially conforms to SRP 13.3, Rev 3, Section II.25, as it includes a technical support center, while the operational support center and emergency operations facility are the responsibility of the Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale design. Other emergency planning requirements, such as NRC notifications, communications, and operational programs, are also designated as the COL applicant’s responsibility.

nuscale_ch01_0601.title: SRP 13.3, Rev 3: Emergency PlanningII.22 ITAAC Associated with Design Certification Application
nuscale_ch01_0601.summary: This section outlines NuScale’s conformance with emergency planning regulatory criteria from SRP 13.3, Rev 3. Most requirements are not applicable to the design certification application and are instead the responsibility of the COL (Combined License) applicant, except for the emergency response data system, which is partially addressed by the NuScale design, with site-specific details left to the COL applicant. Guidance related to early site permits is also noted as not applicable for the design certification stage.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale reactor pressurizer, specifying the alloys and standards for each component, such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting. It emphasizes the use of corrosion-resistant materials compliant with ASME BPVC Section II specifications to ensure compatibility and durability in the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, with leakage rates monitored through RCS inventory balance and containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, and operational limits set in technical specifications. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces moisture, aids in leak detection, and limits oxygen, thereby minimizing corrosion and combustible gas risks. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, significantly lowering the likelihood of core damage and large-break loss-of-coolant accidents compared to traditional nuclear plants.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The NuScale reactor’s containment vessel (CNV) is kept under vacuum during normal operation, which reduces moisture and corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits oxygen for safety. The design relies on passive safety features—such as natural cooling and elimination of external coolant loops—which lowers the risk of large-break LOCAs and results in a lower core damage frequency compared to traditional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0411.title: SRP 9.5.5, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Cooling Water SystemAll (II.1 thru II.7)Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, GDC 17, GDC 44, GDC 45, and GDC 46
nuscale_ch01_0411.summary: The NuScale plant design does not include or require safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the associated systems (fuel oil storage, cooling water, starting, lubrication, and air intake/exhaust) and their compliance with relevant General Design Criteria (GDC) are not applicable. The plant’s safety functions do not rely on either AC or DC power sources.

edges.description: Section A provides an overview of containment features, while Section B gives detailed information about the primary containment's role and limitations in ECCS operation, with both sections referencing the same regulatory criteria and design features for NuScale's containment system.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features and their partial conformance to regulatory criteria, while Section B details how specific containment features function during ECCS operation, referencing the absence of certain systems and the behavior of ECCS water within containment—thus, Section B specifies operational details that elaborate on the general overview in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features and their conformance with regulatory criteria, while Section B specifies detailed requirements for containment water chemistry during ECCS operation, with both sections connected by their discussion of how NuScale's containment design addresses or deviates from standard regulatory expectations for fission product control and water management.

Section A provides a general overview of the containment's compliance with regulatory criteria, specifically noting the absence of certain fission product control systems, while Section B details how the containment functions during ECCS operation by describing the flow of steam and water within the containment vessel, thus Section B specifies the operational details that relate to the general features described in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features and their regulatory conformance, while Section B details how specific containment components (ECCS valves) function and are tested to support containment's role in emergency cooling, thus Section B provides operational specifics that elaborate on the general containment overview in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the containment features, noting the absence of certain systems, while Section B gives detailed requirements for containment performance during ECCS operation, both referencing the specific containment systems present or absent in the NuScale design.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features and their regulatory conformance, while Section B details specific containment functions (such as pH control and hydrogen generation) during ECCS operation, with both sections connected by their discussion of how containment design supports ECCS performance and regulatory criteria.

Section A provides an overview of containment features and notes the absence of a containment spray system in the NuScale design, while Section B details how the ECCS operates within this containment, specifically describing how steam is vented and condensed inside the containment during ECCS operation, thus connecting the general containment overview to its specific role in ECCS function.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features and their regulatory conformance, while Section B gives detailed test results and operational specifics of ECCS valves, illustrating how the containment's design supports ECCS operation as outlined in the overview.

Section A references the regulatory requirements for containment water chemistry during ECCS operation and notes how the NuScale design partially conforms due to differences in containment features, while Section B provides the detailed design and function of the ECCS and how water collects in the containment, thus illustrating how the general overview in Section A is specified in detail by Section B.

Section A provides an overview of containment features relevant to ECCS operation, specifically noting deviations from traditional containment spray systems, while Section B details the ECCS valve design and testing, both sections connecting through their focus on how containment design supports ECCS function in the NuScale reactor.

Section A provides a general overview of the ECCS containment features and their function during a LOCA, while Section B offers detailed technical information and test results confirming the operability of specific ECCS valves described in Section A.

Section A provides an overview of containment features and their role in limiting radiological consequences during a LOCA, while Section B details the specific containment structures and systems (including the absence of fission product clean-up systems) that support or limit the containment's effectiveness in ECCS operation; thus, Section B specifies the technical implementation that underpins the general containment functions described in Section A.

Section A discusses the calculation of radiological consequences from containment leakage during a loss-of-coolant accident, while Section B details the containment's role in controlling fission products, both addressing how containment features impact radiological safety during ECCS operation.

Section A provides an overview of how containment leakage affects radiological consequences during a LOCA, while Section B details the containment's pressure and water chemistry roles during ECCS operation, both connecting through their focus on containment's function in accident mitigation and regulatory conformance.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features and their regulatory conformance, while Section B specifies detailed requirements for containment pressure modeling and water chemistry during ECCS operation, with both sections connected by their focus on containment's role in ECCS performance and regulatory compliance.

Section A provides an overview of containment features and their role in radiological dose calculations, while Section B details the programmatic requirements for containment performance during ECCS operation, with both sections referencing the calculation of radiological consequences and containment leakage as a connecting point.

Section A provides a general overview of emergency response facility design, including containment features, while Section B gives detailed information on the primary containment's role and limitations in fission product control, specifically noting the absence of certain systems relevant to ECCS operation; thus, Section B specifies technical details that elaborate on the general overview in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features and emergency response facilities, while Section B details how the containment supports ECCS operation by describing specific containment pressure and water chemistry requirements, thus Section B specifies the operational role and technical details that elaborate on the general features described in Section A.

Section B provides detailed technical specifications of containment-related components (pressurizer materials and design) that elaborate on the general containment overview referenced in Section A, connecting the high-level emergency planning context to specific containment features relevant for ECCS operation.

Section A specifies the materials and components of the pressurizer, which are part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary discussed in Section B's context of leakage detection within containment.

Section A provides a general overview of the containment vessel's vacuum maintenance and its safety benefits, while Section B details how the containment design (specifically the absence of fission product clean-up and pressure suppression systems) aligns or partially conforms with regulatory criteria for ECCS operation, connecting the general features in A to the regulatory evaluation in B.

Section A provides an overview of the containment vessel's vacuum maintenance and its safety benefits, while Section B details how the containment's design (lacking fission product clean-up and pressure suppression systems) partially conforms to regulatory criteria, thus Section A's general features set the context for Section B's specific regulatory evaluation.

Section A provides a general overview of the containment vessel's vacuum and its safety benefits, while Section B details how the containment's unique features (such as lack of spray system and sump) affect ECCS operation and regulatory conformance, thus Section A's general design features are specified in more detail in Section B's operational context.

Section A provides an overview of the containment vessel's vacuum features and their benefits, while Section B details how the containment design (lacking certain fission product control systems) partially conforms to regulatory criteria, connecting both sections through the containment's functional role in safety and regulatory compliance.

Section A describes the containment vessel's vacuum features that minimize corrosion and leakage, which supports Section B's explanation that the NuScale design does not require traditional fission product clean-up or pressure suppression systems due to its unique containment approach.

Section A describes how maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV) minimizes moisture and corrosion, which supports the containment's reliability, while Section B discusses how the NuScale containment design, lacking a spray system or sump, still collects ECCS water inside the containment during ECCS operation, connecting the general containment features to their specific role in ECCS performance.

Section A provides an overview of containment-related safety system compliance and design philosophy (notably the lack of reliance on emergency diesel generators), while Section B details how the containment structure specifically addresses fission product control and highlights design differences (such as the absence of clean-up systems), thus Section A's general containment features set the context for the specific containment roles described in Section B.

Section A describes the overall compliance of the NuScale design with regulatory criteria for emergency power and cooling systems, while Section B details the specific containment features relevant to fission product control, with both sections connecting through their discussion of how containment design choices (e.g., lack of certain systems) impact the plant's safety systems and regulatory conformance.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features and their compliance with regulatory criteria, while Section B details how the containment's pressure and water chemistry specifically support ECCS operation in the NuScale design, connecting the general design overview to its specific functional role during ECCS events.

Section A and Section B are identical excerpts describing the containment vessel (CNV) vacuum and its benefits, so the overview in Ch.01 is directly referenced or repeated in the detailed discussion in Ch.05.

Section A provides a general overview of containment vessel features (such as vacuum maintenance) that support safety, while Section B details how these features contribute to compliance with regulatory criteria for emergency systems, specifically noting that the containment design eliminates the need for certain emergency systems (like diesel generators) by not relying on AC/DC power for safety functions.

Section A provides a general overview of containment features such as maintaining a vacuum to enhance safety and reliability, while Section B details how these containment features support the plant's safety systems by eliminating the need for emergency diesel generators in ECCS operation, thus showing that the design approach in A satisfies the regulatory requirements discussed in B.
